data.title: Astera Labs Director Jack R Lazar Sells 22,500 Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameLazar Jack R
RelationshipDirector
TypeSell
Amount$4,014,397
SEC FilingForm 4

Astera Labs Director, Jack R Lazar, sold 22,500 shares of Common Stock on August 27, 2025, under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan. The sales were made at weighted average prices ranging from $176.7342 to $183.0903, resulting in a total sale amount of $4,014,397. Following these transactions, Lazar directly owns 97,351 shares of Astera Labs.

SEC Filing: Astera Labs, Inc. [ ALAB ] - Form 4 - Aug. 28, 2025

data.title: Japan July jobless rate falls to 2.3%
data.description:
Japan's jobless rate in July fell to 2.3% from 2.5% in June, government data showed on Friday.

Economists' median forecast was also for 2.5%.

The jobs-to-applicants ratio in July stayed the same from the previous month at 1.22. The median forecast was 1.23.

Adairs Price Target Raised 21% to A$2.60/Share by Bell Potter

Beacon Lighting's Sales Growth Set to Accelerate — Market Talk
data.description: Beacon Lighting's sales are set to accelerate, according to Citi. More green shoots are visible in the Australian housing market, analyst Sam Teeger says. They include less vendor discounting, improved house price growth in July, and clearance rates exceeding historical averages in recent weeks. This activity is being driven by falling interest rates. Australia's central bank has cut rates three times this year and Citi anticipates an additional two reductions by the end of December. Together, this should be positive for Beacon's sales. "Furthermore, Citi's bullish outlook on the consumer from higher spending capacity should also support sales in FY 2026," the bank says. "We forecast like-for-like sales growth of 3% in FY 2026, which is an acceleration from 1.5% in FY 2025." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Australia's Star Entertainment reports narrower annual loss
data.description:
Australia's Star Entertainment reported a narrower annual loss on Friday, helped by a reduced one-off cost, even as remediation costs and subdued patron spending continued to weigh on its performance.

The casino operator reported a statutory net loss after tax of A$471.5 million ($306.38 million) for the year ended June 30, compared to A$1.69 billion a year earlier.

The result fell short of the consensus estimate of a A$244.5 million loss, according to data from Visible Alpha.

The company's lackluster performance highlighted ongoing regulatory challenges since 2021, with authorities investigating potential breaches of anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing laws.

Star said soft trading conditions persisted through July, particularly at its Star Sydney property, where the casino licence remains suspended until at least September 30.

Star Sydney, the firm's flagship property, logged an operating loss of A$86.3 million.

($1 = 1.5389 Australian dollars)

data.title: Corn basis bids mixed as futures climb
data.description:
Spot basis bids for corn were mixed in the U.S. Midwest on Thursday as futures rebounded and farmers sold off some of their old-crop corn to make way for the Autumn harvest.

  • The corn basis declined at a processor in Decatur, Illinois but rose at a Chicago, Illinois-area elevator. It fell at a Davenport, Iowa river terminal, but rose at a Columbus, Ohio rail location.

  • The soy basis declined at a Toledo, Ohio river terminal and was steady elsewhere in a spot check of locations.

  • The USDA reported weekly export sales of soybeans at 1,183,400 metric tons.

  • The USDA reported weekly export sales of corn at 2,071,900 metric tons.

  • Corn and soybeans recovered after falling the previous day, pressured by large global supplies.

Validator withdrawals fuel $30 billion migration into Ethereum liquid restaking protocols
data.description:
Ethereum liquid restaking protocols have experienced remarkable growth, with total value locked across platforms reaching $30 billion, representing a significant shift in how validators are deploying their ETH for yield generation. This surge comes as native ETH staking has seen increased withdrawal activity, with validators exiting the traditional staking mechanism in favor of more dynamic opportunities.

Leading protocols like EtherFi and Eigenpie have captured substantial market share since the beginning of the year, with the sector showing consistent upward momentum through mid-2025.

The migration from native staking to liquid restaking reflects evolving risk appetite and yield optimization strategies among ETH holders. While some market observers initially interpreted validator withdrawals as bearish sentiment, the data suggest users are simply reallocating capital toward more lucrative DeFi opportunities rather than exiting the ecosystem entirely.

Liquid restaking offers the dual benefit of maintaining exposure to ETH staking rewards while providing additional yield opportunities and maintaining liquidity through tradeable receipt tokens. The timing of this shift aligns with broader market conditions and the DeFi ecosystem, delivering promising yield. 

During periods of market turbulence, native ETH staking represented the safest yield-generating option for risk-averse holders seeking steady returns. However, as market conditions have stabilized and DeFi protocols have demonstrated greater resilience, participants get increasingly comfortable exploring higher-yield alternatives that liquid restaking protocols provide.

This trend suggests that as the Ethereum ecosystem continues to evolve, users are becoming more sophisticated in their yield farming strategies, moving beyond simple staking toward more complex but potentially rewarding DeFi positions.

This is an excerpt from The Block's Data & Insights newsletter. Dig into the numbers making up the industry's most thought-provoking trends.

Ulta Beauty Lifts Fiscal-Year Guidance as 2Q Profit, Sales Rise - Update
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Ulta Beauty boosted its guidance for the fiscal year after logging higher profit and sales in its latest quarter as consumers continue to prioritize beauty and wellness products.

The cosmetics and fragrances retailer said Thursday it now expects earnings per share of $23.85 to $24.30, compared with its prior guidance of $22.65 to $23.20.

The Bolingbrook, Ill., company now forecasts sales of $12 billion to $12.1 billion for the year, up from its prior guidance for $11.5 billion to $11.7 billion. It expects same-store sales growth of 2.5% to 3.5%, up from flat to 1.5% previously.

The updated guidance comes as Ulta logged improved results in its latest quarter, lifted by growth across all major categories and market-share growth, according to Chief Executive Kecia Steelman. Engagement with beauty and wellness remains healthy, offering a sense of comfort and escape amid economic uncertainty, she said during a call with analysts.

Those trends should continue to support the category's resilience, she said, even as consumers remain watchful of tariff-driven price increases.

The outlook also reflects caution around how consumer demand may evolve in the second half of the year, Steelman said. "While near-term uncertainty persists, we're staying focused on what we can control," she said.

Longer term, Ulta expects the end of its four-year partnership with Target to provide a boost as it draws customers into its wholly-owned stores, Steelman said. The partnership, set to end next August, brought Ulta's products to Target's stores and websites, generating royalty revenue well below 1% of net sales over fiscal 2024, Steelman said.

"I think there's going to be a huge upside potential opportunity for us to really recapture those guests back into the Ulta Beauty ecosystem," she said.

For the second quarter, the company posted a profit of $260.9 million, or $5.78 a share, compared with $252.6 million, or $5.30 a share, a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet expected $5.10 in earnings a share.

Revenue rose 9.3%, to $2.79 billion, topping analyst projections for $2.68 billion.

Same-store sales rose 6.7%, beating the 2.9% increase Wall Street was expecting. Increases in average ticket and number of transactions helped drive the growth, the company said.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Fed's Waller backs a series of interest-rate cuts over the next six months
data.description:
By Greg Robb

Fed governor doesn't favor a 50-basis-point cut in September, at least not yet

Federal Reserve governor Christopher Waller wanted the Fed to lower rates last month.

Federal Reserve governor Christopher Waller on Thursday said he now supports a series of interest-rate cuts beginning in September, with the pace of subsequent moves driven by the incoming data.

Waller had urged the Fed to cut rates at its last meeting in July, and he said he is more sure now that it was the right call.

"Let's get on with it," Waller said Thursday at an event in Miami.

The White House has said Waller is among the list of candidates who will be interviewed for the job to replace Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, when Powell's term ends in May.

President Donald Trump has been insisting for months that the Fed should be lowering rates quickly. He has belittled Powell almost daily and publicly toyed with the idea of firing the Fed chief.

Earlier this week, Trump took the unprecedented step of firing a sitting Fed governor, Lisa Cook, seeking to overturn laws and traditions that have kept the central bank independent from White House control. Analysts said the move is primarily aimed at giving Trump the opportunity to put supporters of his policy goals on the Fed's interest-rate committee. Cook has gone to court to block her firing, calling it unlawful.

In his remarks to the Economic Club of Miami on Thursday, Waller didn't mention Trump or Cook. The Fed governor said he favors rate cuts because he's worried the labor market is weaker than it looks on the surface.

Waller also said that inflation from tariffs will be temporary and therefore the Fed doesn't need to hold rates higher than normal to guard against another outbreak of steadily rising prices.

Over the first seven months of the year, Powell backed a policy of keeping rates higher than normal to guard against an outbreak of inflation, which has been above the Fed's target for four years.

In his speech last week in Jackson Hole, Wyo., Powell opened the door for a rate cut in September, but stressed the Fed needed to ease carefully.

It is possible other Fed officials may dissent from a rate cut in September. Only two months ago, seven Fed officials said they thought the Fed should not make any cuts this year. Only one other Fed official, Fed governor Michelle Bowman, backed Waller's call for a July rate cut.

The Fed's benchmark interest rate is now in a range of 4.25% -4.5%. A survey of Fed officials indicates that a normal, or "neutral," setting of rates is around 3%. That is the estimated level where rates are not boosting growth or dampening activity.

Using that math, Waller said interest rates can be 125-150 basis points lower than the current setting.

"The totality of the data and the outlook will determine how quickly I believe we need to move policy to a neutral setting, but as I stand here today, I anticipate additional cuts over the next three to six months, and the pace of rate cuts will be driven by the incoming data," Waller said.

Waller added he doesn't currently support a 50-basis-point cut at the Fed's meeting on Sept. 16-17, but added he might change his mind if the August jobs report points to a substantially weakening economy and inflation remains well-contained. The jobs data will be released on Sept. 5, with the inflation data coming the following week.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Tokyo Aug core CPI rises 2.5% yr/yr
data.description:
TOKYO, Aug 29 (Reuters) - Core consumer prices in Tokyo rose 2.5% in August from a year earlier, government data showed on Friday.

The core consumer price index for Japan’s capital, which includes oil products but excludes fresh food prices, compared with economists' median estimate for a 2.5% annual rise.

(Reporting by Tokyo Newsroom)

data.title: Fed's Waller says 'no set sequence' on coming rate cuts
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller on Thursday said there is "no set sequence" to the rate cuts he expects the U.S. central bank will deliver in coming months, starting next month.

"The data will kind of determine how fast we go: it could be a sequence of cuts; it may be a couple, then you may want to pause," Waller said at the Economic Club of Miami.

"We know we want to head towards neutral; it's just a question how fast we get there."

data.title: Virgin Australia reports 28% rise in annual profit
data.description:
Virgin Australia reported a near 28% rise in its full-year underlying profit on Friday, in its first result since relisting, driven by strong performance in its airline and loyalty program segments.

Virgin, which is Australia's second-largest airline by market share after Qantas Airways , was delisted in 2020 after private equity giant Bain Capital rescued it from administration.

The company returned to the Australian Securities Exchange in June this year after an IPO, which valued it at A$2.32 billion on a fully diluted basis.

Underlying earnings at the company's Airlines segment grew more than 36% in the year ended June 30, as it was able to fill more seats with an improvement in passenger mix.

Meanwhile, its loyalty program, Velocity, also saw a 10.5% rise in earnings on the back of growth in the active member base.

The airline said its pro forma underlying net profit after tax stood at A$331 million ($215.08 million) for the year, in line with the forecast it provided at the time of its IPO.

($1 = 1.5389 Australian dollars)

data.title: Star Entertainment Says Activity Stabilizes, But Trading Conditions Remain Soft
data.description:
(23:11 GMT) *Star Entertainment FY Net Loss A$471.5 Million
(23:12 GMT) *Star Entertainment FY Revenue A$1.36 Billion, Down 19%
(23:12 GMT) *Star Entertainment Says Activity Stabilized in 2H of FY 2025
(23:13 GMT) *Star Entertainment Says 3Q, 4Q Revenue Broadly Consistent at Depressed Levels
(23:13 GMT) *Star Entertainment Says Soft Trading Conditions Continued in July
(23:13 GMT) *Star Entertainment Says Soft Trading Conditions in July Particularly at Sydney Casino
(23:14 GMT) *Star Entertainment Says July Revenue of A$92.1 Million Slightly Ahead of 4Q Average
(23:14 GMT) *Star Entertainment Says Ebitda Loss of A$7.4 Million Slightly Ahead of 4Q Average
(23:14 GMT) *Star Entertainment Had A$234 Million Available Cash at End-June
(23:15 GMT) *Star Entertainment Had Closing Available Cash of A$189 Million at August 25
(23:15 GMT) *Star Entertainment: August 25 Cash Result Includes Payment of A$16M of Historical Deferred Casino Taxes
(23:16 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Material Uncertainty About Ability to Continue as Going Concern Remains
(23:24 GMT) Star Entertainment Says Activity Stabilizes, But Trading Conditions Remain Soft

By David Winning

SYDNEY--The Star Entertainment Group said recent trading has stabilized but conditions remain soft, as the Australian casino operator again said it remains uncertain whether it can continue as a going concern.

Star said its trading performance deteriorated in the 12 months through June, driving a statutory net loss for the period of 471.5 million Australian dollars (US$308 million). The result included A$212.4 million of significant items. Annual revenue fell by 19% to A$1.36 billion.

Still, Star said activity in the second half of fiscal 2025 had stabilized. Revenue was broadly consistent in the third and fourth quarters of the period, albeit at depressed levels.

"Soft trading conditions continued during July 2025, particularly at The Star Sydney," Star said in a regulatory filing on Friday. "July group revenue of A$92.1 million and Ebitda loss of A$7.4 million were slightly ahead of the 4Q monthly average in fiscal 2025.

Star said it ended fiscal 2025 with available cash of A$234 million. By the start of this week, this had fallen to A$189 million, including the impact of paying some A$16 million of historical deferred casino taxes, the company said.

Write to David Winning at david.winning@wsj.com

data.title: S. Korea Retail Sales Growth Hits Over 2-Year High
data.description:
South Korea’s retail sales surged 2.5% month-over-month in July 2025, accelerating from a revised 0.7% increase in June and marking the fastest growth in over two years.

Sales rose across categories, led by durable goods (+5.4%) such as telecommunications equipment and computers, semi-durable goods (+2.7%) including clothing and recreational items, and non-durable goods (+1.1%) such as food, pharmaceuticals, and stationery.

Retail sales are a key gauge of private consumption, providing insights into consumer behavior and broader economic sentiment.

data.title: Gap insists it's 'getting its vibe back.' But Wall Street's tariff worries got in the way.
data.description:
By Bill Peters

Same-store sales growth of 1% came in below expectations

Shares of Gap Inc. slid after hours on Thursday after the clothing retailer reported second-quarter sales trends and a third-quarter forecast that disappointed Wall Street.

The results follow subdued expectations for the quarter, as the company searches for stronger gains at its Banana Republic and Athleta stores, and as other chains - such as Urban Outfitters Inc. (URBN) and department-store chain Kohl's Corp. (KSS) - have shown signs of turning things around.

Shares of Gap (GAP,) which also owns Old Navy, were down 6.5% after hours.

Gap reported $3.725 billion in sales for the quarter, compared with Wall Street's expectations for $3.733 billion. Same-store sales rose 1%, below analyst estimates for a 2% gain. Earnings of 57 cents a share topped forecasts for 55 cents.

The company said it expects third-quarter sales growth of 1.5% to 2.5, roughly in line with FactSet estimates. Gap stuck with its full-year outlook. Management said the forecasts included the estimated impact of the latest tariff rates, which have threatened to raise prices on already inflation-weary consumers.

-Bill Peters

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

South Korea Industrial Output Misses Forecasts
data.description:
South Korea’s industrial production grew 0.3% month-over-month in July 2025, easing from an upwardly revised 1.7% increase in June and falling short of market expectations of 0.5%.

Output was weighed down by sharp declines in automobile (-7.3%) and semiconductor (-3.6%) production, though overall manufacturing still edged up 0.2%.

Strong gains were seen in electronic components (+20.9%) and machinery (+6.5%).

On a yearly basis, industrial production jumped 5% in July, accelerating from 1.6% growth in June.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1910 ET [Dow Jones]--Wesfarmers's key retail businesses have more sales momentum than Citi expected, prompting the bank to upgrade its stock to neutral. Wesfarmers's Bunnings home-improvements chain is benefiting from an expanded tool shop, which Citi expects will be a meaningful driver of sales growth in FY 2026. "We now expect Bunnings to generate Ebit growth of 9% in FY 2026 (was 4%) on higher sales growth and improved operating leverage," analyst Adrian Lemme says. Similarly, Citi sees discount department-store chain Kmart holding its own even as consumer spending picks up. It now forecasts 10% Ebit growth in Kmart, compared to 4% before. "While the valuation is high, we no longer see an obvious earnings catalyst to warrant a sell rating considering our bullish view on consumer spending into FY 2026," Citi says. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1903 ET [Dow Jones]--Lifestyle Communities has largely eliminated concerns about its balance sheet, and gets a new bull in Citi as a result. Analyst Suraj Nebhani says investors had been concerned about its interest cover ratio covenant of more than 1.75x heading into this week's annual earnings result. Lifestyle Communities said debt peaked at A$490 million in May, and is now trending down. It expects to shave around A$100 million more off its debt over the six months through December. "We believe that with the debt reductions, Lifestyle Communities may be able to be comfortable on covenants even with annual settlements at 200," Citi says. It upgrades Lifestyle Communities to buy, from neutral. "We also remove high risk rating given we no longer see covenant/capital raising risk," Citi says. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1848 ET - Jefferies is a big fan of everything about car dealership Eargers Automotive--except its valuation. Eagers's share price rose 12% on Thursday after beating expectations in 1H. Revenue of A$6.5 billion was 7% ahead of consensus hopes, while gross profit was 2% ahead of estimates. Jefferies says Eagers controls 14% of new vehicle sales in Australia, compared to the largest U.S. dealer, which has 2% of the market there. It's also big in used-vehicle sales. That makes it attractive for auto makers looking to establish a retail presence in Australia. Still, Jefferies says Eagers's valuation metrics following Thursday's short squeeze just look too high. "The market appears to be pricing in deals yet to happen," says analyst John Campbell. "We can't get there." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1837 ET [Dow Jones]--Private hospital operator Ramsay Health Care expects earnings in Australia to rise in FY 2026. Jefferies thinks any improvement will be modest due to labor cost pressures. "Ramsay seen significant wage rises from all public payers, which likely has flow-on effects for the private sector," analyst David Stanton says. Wage bargaining negotiations have been finalized in New South Wales and Western Australia states, which represent 27% of group Ebit. Deals remain outstanding in Queensland and Victoria, also representing 27% of Ebit. Also, a new contract for Joondalup, Western Australia, will deal a A$37 million hit to Ebit. Jefferies retains a hold call on Ramsay, cuts its price target by 11% to A$34.50/share. Ramsay ended Thursday at A$34.09. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1754 ET - Caterpillar raises its expected cost for tariffs this year after hundreds of imported finished goods containing steel and aluminum were made eligible for 50% U.S. duties on the metal in them earlier this month. The construction equipment maker now expects to pay $1.5 billion to $1.8 billion in 2025, up from $1.3 billion to $1.5 billion expected earlier. The company says its 3Q tariffs expenses will be about $500 million to $600 million. The revised tariff outlook is not expected to affect the Caterpillar's sales forecast. Shares down 3.6% in after-hours trading. (robert.tita@wsj.com)

1625 ET - The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board postpones implementation of tighter rules on auditors' quality controls following pushback from audit firms. The rule will take effect on Dec. 15, 2026 instead of Dec. 15 this year, the audit watchdog says. The PCAOB adopted the rule in May 2024, followed by SEC approval. Firms will have to assess the risks associated with achieving effective quality controls and design procedures intended to address those risks. The Center of Audit Quality, which represents firms that audit public companies, asked to delay the implementation to allow firms more time to prepare. The SEC, which oversees the PCAOB, is seeking candidates for all five board seats, including the chair. Erica Williams resigned as PCAOB chair last month at SEC Chair Paul Atkins' request.(mark.maurer@wsj.com; @markgmaurer)

1605 ET - U.S. stocks end higher following a stronger-than-expected gains in 2Q GDP and a decline in weekly jobless claims. Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook sues President Trump, seeking to block his move to fire her. Nvidia slips 0.8% after posting quarterly results Wednesday. Dick's Sporting Goods shares fall 4.8% despite strong earnings and raises full-year guidance. Communication services and technology sectors drive the gains, while utility stocks fall the most. DJIA rises71 points, or 0.2%, to 45636, and the S&P 500 gains 0.3% to 6501, both new records, while the Nasdaq climbs 0.5% to 21705. (patrick.sullivan@wsj.com)

1448 ET - The number of replacement heifers available to enter the dairy herd as milk producing cows has fallen to a 20-year low, and replacements could fall even further, according to CoBank. A report from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange says the national dairy heifer shortage could persist and grow deeper in the next two years, which would limit growth in the milk supply, before a recovery begins in 2027. At the same time, CoBank said, the U.S. is experiencing an historic $10 billion investment in new dairy processing facilities expected to come online through 2027. According to CoBank, the incentive to produce calves destined for the beef supply stems from a contraction in the U.S. cattle supply, which currently stands at a 75-year low, and which has helped drive beef prices to record highs. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1444 ET - Kohl's second-quarter results are further evidence the department-store chain is losing share to other retail channels, including off-price retailers, UBS analysts say in a note. While the results were better than expected, Kohl's same-store sales declined for the fifteenth consecutive quarter, and with the company in the early stages of a turnaround plan, there likely won't be any meaningful improvement until at least 2026, the analysts say. They expect Wall Street's earnings per-share estimates to move lower over the next year given weak sales, tariff-related cost increases and an increase in promotional offers during the coming holiday season compared to the prior year, keeping sentiment muted. Shares fall 6.1% to $15.19. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1421 ET - Cooper Cos.'s solid margins and improving free cash flow should help the company's stock despite the company's poor third-quarter earnings, say Stifel analysts, who label the stock as a "buy." The company's adjusted operating margin of 26.1% beat Wall Street estimates and Cooper said operating efficiencies are expected to continue through a restructuring in 2026. Free cash flow also beat expectations by a wide margin with working-capital efficiencies expected to continue bolstering the company's cash in coming years. The stock is down 11%, to $66.27, Thursday. That decline gives investors an opportunity to buy, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1416 ET - Jack Daniel's Tennessee Whiskey maker Brown-Forman sees weakness in the U.S. market for spirits as slightly more cyclical than structural, CEO Lawson Whiting says on a call with analysts. Inflation and high interest rates are still hurting customers' ability to spend, and tariffs are putting pressure on them as well, Whiting says. Structural considerations-like the rising popularity of cannabis, more use of GLP-1 drugs and a general wellness push among consumers--wouldn't be able to tank the alcohol market as quickly as has been seen, the CEO says. "There is some continuum between cyclical and structural," he says. "But I still feel like the cyclical side is a little bit bigger piece of it." Shares retreat 5.1% to $28.89. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1329 ET - Five Below shoppers are responding well to the discount retailer's price increases, UBS analyst Michael Lasser says. Normally, customers would gradually start buying or visiting less after a retailer makes mass price changes, Lasser says. But Five Below has actually received more visits and spending over the past few months since it initiated price hikes in April and May in response to tariffs. Lasser thinks that's because customers are recognizing the widening price gaps between Five Below and other retailers, as Five Below still hasn't passed the full cost of tariffs down to sticker prices. That leaves Five Below with room to potentially raise prices further. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Star Entertainment Says Soft Trading Conditions in July Particularly at Sydney Casino
Star Entertainment Says Soft Trading Conditions Continued in July
Star Entertainment Says 3Q, 4Q Revenue Broadly Consistent at Depressed Levels
